Exploring Palmer Penguins

Sofia Rodriguez

Introduction

This report explores the Palmer Penguins dataset. I will examine differences among penguin species, sexes, and island locations using summary tables and graphs.

Exploratory Analysis

# Examine the beginning of the penguins dataframe.
head(penguins)
## # A tibble: 6 × 8
##   species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
##   <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
## 1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
## 2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
## 3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
## 4 Adelie  Torgersen           NA            NA                  NA          NA
## 5 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
## 6 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
## # ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

Dataset size

The following code checks how many columns and penguin observations are in the dataset.

# Count the columns and rows in the penguins dataframe.
ncol(penguins)
## [1] 8
nrow(penguins)
## [1] 344

Representation by sex

The following code checks whether male and female penguins are represented equally in the dataset.

# Count male, female, and missing sex values.
table(penguins$sex, useNA = "ifany")
## 
## female   male   <NA> 
##    165    168     11
# Create dataframes containing only male or female penguins.
pmale <- penguins[
  !is.na(penguins$sex) & penguins$sex == "male",
]

pfemale <- penguins[
  !is.na(penguins$sex) & penguins$sex == "female",
]

# Confirm the number of rows in each dataframe.
nrow(pmale)
## [1] 168
nrow(pfemale)
## [1] 165

Representation by species

The following code checks whether each penguin species is represented equally in the dataset.

# Count the penguins in each species.
table(penguins$species, useNA = "ifany")
## 
##    Adelie Chinstrap    Gentoo 
##       152        68       124
# Create one dataframe for each species.
pAdel <- penguins[penguins$species == "Adelie", ]
pChin <- penguins[penguins$species == "Chinstrap", ]
pGen <- penguins[penguins$species == "Gentoo", ]

# Confirm the number of rows in each dataframe.
nrow(pAdel)
## [1] 152
nrow(pChin)
## [1] 68
nrow(pGen)
## [1] 124

Representation by island

The following code checks whether each island is represented equally in the dataset.

# Count the penguins observed on each island.
table(penguins$island, useNA = "ifany")
## 
##    Biscoe     Dream Torgersen 
##       168       124        52
# Create one dataframe for each island.
iBis <- penguins[penguins$island == "Biscoe", ]
iDream <- penguins[penguins$island == "Dream", ]
iTorg <- penguins[penguins$island == "Torgersen", ]

# Confirm the number of rows in each dataframe.
nrow(iBis)
## [1] 168
nrow(iDream)
## [1] 124
nrow(iTorg)
## [1] 52

Data Visualization

Measurements that distinguish male and female penguins

Body mass may help distinguish male and female penguins. The plot below compares body mass by sex while separating the three species.

# Remove rows with missing sex or body-mass values before plotting.
penguins_sex <- penguins[
  !is.na(penguins$sex) & !is.na(penguins$body_mass_g),
]

ggplot(
  data = penguins_sex,
  aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g, fill = sex)
) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Penguin Body Mass by Sex",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    fill = "Sex"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

Within each species, male penguins generally have greater body mass than female penguins. Separating the plot by species helps prevent species size differences from confusing the comparison.

Measurements that distinguish penguin species

Bill length and bill depth may help distinguish the three penguin species.

# Compare bill measurements among the penguin species.
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  aes(
    x = bill_length_mm,
    y = bill_depth_mm,
    color = species
  )
) +
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.75,
    size = 2,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Bill Measurements by Penguin Species",
    x = "Bill length (mm)",
    y = "Bill depth (mm)",
    color = "Species"
  ) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

The species form different groups based on bill measurements. Adelie penguins generally have shorter bills, Chinstrap penguins generally have longer bills, and Gentoo penguins generally have shallower bills.

Penguin species observed at each island

The following bar chart compares the penguin species observed at Biscoe, Dream, and Torgersen islands.

# Count species observations at each island.
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  aes(x = island, fill = species)
) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Penguin Species Observed at Each Island",
    x = "Island",
    y = "Number of penguins",
    fill = "Species"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

Adelie penguins were observed on all three islands. Chinstrap penguins were observed at Dream Island, while Gentoo penguins were observed at Biscoe Island.
